questions.prompt: What problem does Contextual Retrieval solve?

choices.text: Chunks losing context when split from their parent document. Prepending a 1–3 sentence context to each chunk before embedding (and indexing for BM25) restores enough information to make retrieval work on cross-referencing queries — dropping failure rates 35–67%.

questions.explanation: Chunking is destructive — a chunk that says 'It was reduced 15% YoY' loses the entity, year, and document context. Contextual Retrieval restores that signal by prepending a short Claude-generated context. The chunk becomes self-describing for retrieval purposes.

questions.prompt: A 500K-token corpus needs Contextual Retrieval. Naive cost looks scary. What's the standard cost-control pattern?

choices.text: Use Haiku for generation and turn on prompt caching for the whole document in the system prompt. Each chunk's generation re-uses the cached document at ~10% cost. Total cost typically drops from hundreds of dollars to a few dollars one-time.
Generate context for only the first chunk of each doc.

questions.explanation: Haiku + prompt caching is the canonical pattern from Anthropic's writeup. Caching makes the whole-document re-send nearly free; Haiku is cheap per call. A 1M-token corpus costs roughly $1 to fully process with context.
